name: FTP Brute Force Flow Pattern
rationale: FTP control traffic commonly uses TCP port 21.
Brute-force-like flows may show repeated short TCP connections with elevated packet rate.
This is a flow-level heuristic and does not inspect FTP login payloads.

name: SSH Brute Force Flow Pattern
rationale: SSH control traffic commonly uses TCP port 22.
SSH brute-force attempts often create repeated short TCP flows against SSH port 22.
This is a flow-level heuristic and cannot inspect SSH authentication results.

name: High Rate DoS Flow Pattern
rationale: DoS-like flows may show high packet rate from one direction.
Limited backward packet count can indicate poor response balance during a high-rate flow.
This threshold is a prototype heuristic and should be calibrated with feature distribution analysis.

name: Distributed High Rate Flow Pattern
rationale: DDoS-like flows may show very high packet rate and strong forward traffic volume.
Low backward packet count can indicate response imbalance under high-rate traffic.
This is a single-flow heuristic and does not prove distributed source behavior by itself.

name: Botnet Beacon-Like Flow Pattern
rationale: Botnet communication can appear as longer-lived beacon-like flows.
Moderate packet rate and forward packet count can indicate repeated command-and-control style communication.
This heuristic does not use destination reputation or host history.

name: Suspicious Web Flow Pattern
rationale: Web attack traffic commonly uses HTTP or HTTPS destination ports.
Larger packet length means can indicate request-heavy or payload-heavy web flows.
This flow-level heuristic cannot inspect URLs, request parameters, SQL strings, or XSS payloads.

name: Long Duration Infiltration-Like Flow Pattern
rationale: Infiltration-like flows may involve longer duration network activity.
Sustained bytes per second and backward packets can indicate interactive or data-transfer behavior.
This heuristic is weak without host context, payload inspection, or post-compromise behavior analysis.
